story:
Mama had been to the hospital many times and always came home, so when she went in on February 4, I believed this time would be the same. Baba even insisted I go to football practice at the Shooting Club in El Dokki, Giza. Captain Abdallah already knew Mama was ill; he greeted me with “Al salam aleikum,” prayed for her, and called her gentle and respectable. My teammate Omar told me I should have said “inshallah” after I said she would return. I didn’t want to obey him, so I stayed quiet and followed the coach’s instructions.

Three days later, I came home and found the door locked. Baba wasn’t there, and I didn’t have a key. Ibrahim and Mahmoud from the garage across the street saw me waiting and called me over. We exchanged “Al salam aleikum,” and Ibrahim asked if I had prayed Dhuhr at school. I hadn’t; I didn’t want to miss the bus. “Don’t worry,” he said. “Asr is in twenty minutes. Let’s pray together.” Mahmoud stayed outside, calm as ever, smoking a thick cigarette that smelled strange. After we prayed, Ibrahim lifted his hands and begged, “Oh Allah, you who shelter us, please cure Mostafa’s mother and allow her to return home with health and strength, and when Teta Sonia collapses at the burial, help Mostafa bring her water.” I asked why he prayed for Mama when he barely knew her. “One needs only empathy to pray for another,” he said. I didn’t fully understand, but I repeated “Amen.”

On February 8, Mama died. It was only the second time I saw Baba cry—the first was when his own mother, Teta Soraya, died. He loved them both. He spent the night praying, reading Quran, and arranging the funeral. He kept telling me to pray for Mama. I didn’t understand how she could be gone when she always came home.

That Friday, I couldn’t sleep and only dozed off after Fajr. I missed Jumu’ah. Baba missed it too. We buried Mama. Seeing her body frightened me. Baba told me to be strong, then he cried too. We read Quran. Teta Sonia collapsed, and I ran to buy her water.

Baba made me go to practice the next day. “Be a man and carry on,” he said. Everyone told me, “Only God lives on.” After practice, Omar stood in the locker room and said my mother died because I didn’t say “inshallah.” He said I had jinxed her by insisting she’d come home. I sat alone on the bench, staring at cockroaches beneath the sinks and counting the cracks in the tiles—ninety-nine, like Allah’s names. Maybe Omar was right, I thought.

I started saying “inshallah” at the end of every sentence. I said “Bismillah” before eating and “Alhamdulillah” after. I ate with my right hand. I put my right shoe on first. I promised to never miss a prayer and to fast Mondays and Thursdays, like the Prophet, inshallah—so God wouldn’t be angry at me or at Mama or hurt Baba.

I prayed more that night, even Shaf and Witr, and asked for forgiveness. I wanted to believe Baba: that God is merciful, that my words don’t control life or death, and that love and prayer—not fear—are how we carry on. I remembered Ibrahim’s lesson: empathy is enough. I held onto that.
